definition: To accept something as it appears without questioning whether it is really true or genuine.
usage_notes: Often used with 'take' — 'take at face value'
Frequently used in negative or cautionary contexts: 'don't take it at face value'
Implies the surface appearance may not match reality

etymology.origin_summary: From the stated value printed on the face of a coin or banknote, which may differ from its actual or market value.

examples: Don't take the sales figures at face value — they include one-time gains.
She took his apology at face value and moved on.

confusions.difference: 'Literally' means exactly as stated; 'at face value' means accepting appearance without deeper investigation.

literal_gloss: The value shown on the surface of a coin or note.
figurative_meaning: Accept something as it appears without questioning the reality beneath.
misuse_notes: Only meaningful when there is reason to suspect the surface view may be incomplete or deceptive.
